title: More than Just Algebra
summary: Mrs. Eggleston discusses teaching in rural New York, the importance of relationships in education, and how small schools shape both students and teachers.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves just over 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on what it means to teach in a place where classes are small enough that teachers know not just how students learn, but who they are outside the classroom as well.

questions.q: How many students does Canaseraga typically graduate in a year?
questions.a: It depends on the year. I think the smallest since I've been here is around nine. A bigger class for us is around 24—that's the biggest class I've had since I've taught. I taught sixth grade for the first eight years that I was here, and then I moved into math education, so this is my third year in the high school.

questions.q: What brought you into teaching, and what brought you to math?
questions.a: Teaching-wise, I think I just always wanted to since I was younger. My mom ran a daycare growing up, so I was always around little kids and taking care of them. My mom does a lot of educational aspects in the daycare, and growing up I was just always the person who loved school, so it kind of made sense. When I originally went to college, I didn't major in education right off the bat—it was math and science, just because those were my preferences. Then I ended up at Cortland for my education. Even then, it really evolves as you go, because I think people have a certain picture of what it means to be a teacher, and once you get into it, it's actually such a broad area—you can do so much. I went for elementary education originally. When this was my very first job outside of subbing and student teaching, I thought for sure second grade was my preference. That was my first placement, and then I moved to fourth grade and looked at that a lot more. When I got hired here it was sixth grade, and I was kind of terrified—middle schoolers might not be my thing—but then I loved it the whole time I was in sixth grade. Math has just always been my preference. The way I ended up in this position was that we had a teacher who left on maternity leave and didn't come back, and then there was a long-term sub in her position. When she left, nobody applied. I had kind of said to my superintendent at the time, wow, I can't believe nobody wants to just teach high school math all day—that's the best. And he was like, oh, don't you do that. Now I'm doing it, and it is the best. I just teach all the algebras—two-year algebra track and all the sections, and I love it.

questions.q: What does day-to-day teaching look like here, especially for someone unfamiliar with a rural setting?
questions.a: I'm from Jasper and I went to Jasper-Troupsburg, so I was familiar with small school, small district—even Canaseraga is smaller than J-T, so I was kind of shocked at that when I applied and got the sixth grade job. You're the only sixth grade teacher, so something that immediately was apparent was you don't have even one other teacher at your grade level to keep pace with or bounce ideas off of or do a project together. You're the whole grade level—now I'm the whole algebra and the whole algebra II. That is sometimes a blessing and a curse. You're not really accountable to somebody else, which can be good, but there are times when it's kind of like I'm doing Regents exam review right now and I wonder, is this the best strategy that I'm using? What do other people do, and I don't really have anybody to ask, so that is a challenge. Day to day, I would say the best part about it and what I think makes small districts more successful is that you cannot help but get to know your kids—even more than you ever want to about them. The current ninth grade was the last class that I had in sixth grade. When I decided to move up to math, I had them again in seventh and again in eighth, and I will have half of them next year for the two-year track and the other half back the following year for algebra II. That's going on five and six years in a row with the same kids. In that particular group, I just know them. I can look at a lesson and instantly say this student is going to fly through this, I'm going to have to make sure they have something else to do at the end, this student is going to struggle on two, five, and 7A—seriously down to the kid, down to the problem. You can anticipate where their mind's going to go, what they're going to write, the mistake they're going to make, how to fix it. Even growing up in J-T, we would have a class size of 20. I have a class size of five a lot of the time, so you really can know their strengths, their weaknesses, you understand their lives outside of school, and how that affects their achievement in school.

questions.q: How much does that personal connection influence students' willingness to keep trying, even when math isn't their strength?
questions.a: A ton. I've always said that from sixth grade. I know in college you spend a lot of time writing lesson plans and all the logistics of things, but you don't get a lot of time spent on building relationships, connections, classroom management—because there is often this trap of teachers who come in and they're new and they want to be liked and they want to form relationships, but the management piece kind of comes at the expense of that. Then you have classes that get wild and crazy because the teacher just wants to be popular versus setting boundaries. Then you have teachers who I think the boundaries are a bit harsh—they're strict, and that prevents the relationship from forming, and then you get cases where it's like, oh, this person's a jerk, I'm not going to do work for them. I think you have to have a middle ground, but if you can establish it, I have kids in here that—you just, I guess I always think of it as I have to earn their respect. Once you have it, they will work much harder for you. Even on days when I'm out, the kids just know how the room is. They know me as well as I know them, so they know my expectations. They know if there's a sub in here and one class leaves the room a mess, they know where to put things and that I'm going to want to come in and the room be clean and I would be upset if it wasn't. They work harder in class for sure when they know—I guess if they can see the effort that's going into the lesson. If they respect you, they'll respect that you worked on that and so then they'll work for you. They'll know Mrs. E is going to be so disappointed if I did bad on this because she knows I can do better, and so they'll do that much more work. You also kind of hear snippets of their conversations—you'll hear them talk about other classes, like I was absent for three days, I'm going to do all this teacher's work first, and I don't care about this teacher's work. Their attitudes about their teachers have an impact. Kids work better for teachers that they like, for sure.

questions.q: For students who ask "when am I ever going to use this?" how do you make math feel more relevant?
questions.a: As the years go on, the fact that you care about them—they know it, and it just becomes more familiar, so it's less present in their mind. The older they get and the higher the stakes get with the exams, the more they're kind of like, well, this is stupid, why do I have to do it? Why do I have to pass this test? So there are times when I'll adjust my curriculum to put more relevant situations in. Math definitely has a stigma for word problems being crazy unrealistic and unrelatable, so I will adjust those and make them about the kids themselves—give them a question, put their name in it, things like that to keep them engaged. One area that is lacking when it comes to rural education is making that connection. For a kid who kind of has their mind made up—well, my dad's a welder and he makes good money, so I'm gonna be a welder when I graduate and I don't need this—I think bringing in more education about careers, and even careers outside of your immediate town where you live, would really help them to see that they would be capable of that. A lot of the kids that I teach aren't thinking about what happens after high school. It's not in their brain. It's either I'm a college kid or I'm a workforce kid. There's no, well, let me actually see what's out there—what are the jobs? Their view of that is so narrow and so localized, and so it is often really hard to get them to see where upper-level algebra, for sure, is going to be relevant in my life, because they just don't have a concept of where that information could actually be useful. A lot of the way that I approach it is kind of like, well, it doesn't matter what you do with your life. At certain times you're going to run into challenges—challenging tasks where you have to think them through, you have to problem solve, you have to do something you don't want to do. For you right now as a 14-year-old, algebra might be one of those things, and you have to still give it your best effort and think it through and solve the problem. If you think you know exactly where your life's going to go and what your job's going to be, you might have to pass a certification exam, work with someone you don't like—there's always going to be a challenge that you have to work through. I try to approach it that way, but I think if they had more of a concept of careers in general outside of town, that would help.

questions.q: You mentioned that smaller schools are more successful—what do you mean by that?
questions.a: Every year we look at our test grades and compare them to the greater BOCES region, and it always comes out that Canaseraga and Bradford—another super small district—the scores are always kind of soaring compared to a lot of bigger schools like Bath or Horseheads or Hornell. Part of that makes sense—at bigger schools you're going to have a wider variety of kids, which is harder to meet the needs there, but you also have a lot more resources. For me in algebra, those Regents scores are only mine compared to Hornell, which might have three different teachers who contributed to those scores—that's a lot more knowledge and resources they can devote and collaborate on versus just me here. Our numbers are skewed because where they might have hundreds of kids taking the same test, I've got 11. It can be good or bad, because if two kids don't do well, then my grades are just not going to look great. But it just seems consistently that the small schools outperform the larger schools, and I think that's because you just have such a smaller chance of falling through the cracks. If I had a class of 24 in here, one or two kids missing school here and there is kind of okay, that's normal. I have a class of five and my one kid isn't here for three days—it's very apparent. Also, I can easily track that kid down and get them caught up one on one. The teachers here are really good about working together on that. There's not many of us, especially in the high school, so communicating—this one kid is currently absent, everyone's kind of fighting for his or her time when we get them caught up. We are really good at working out systems for that. Physically being present is noticeable. If I have a class of five kids, I know them very well, and I can easily pinpoint where they're struggling and give them more time and attention than I could if I had a much bigger group. The advantage is a lot more one-on-one time with your teacher—centralized help specific to you.

questions.q: What role do very small classes—sometimes as few as five students—play in how students engage with each other and with the material?
questions.a: It's interesting to look at the dynamics between the five kids that I have in here at a time versus when the global teacher has the whole class together. She sees I have two students that always want to work together, always help each other. It's kind of like a friendly competition-based thing, but they'll help each other too. But in global, they don't even speak to each other. I definitely see it in here. They form these miniature friendships and bonds that might not exist outside of the algebra classroom, but in here they will help each other and work together. I kind of like the competition part of it—they're not competing for anything, it's really more with each other, but they'll push each other, which I appreciate. I kind of feel like that's also a downside with a small school like this—usually you'll have one or two kids who are way top of their class and everyone else is miles behind, and some of those kids kind of just feel like, I'm just not a smart kid, I'm just not top of my class, and they kind of give up. But when I have them in here in a really small class, they will really compete with each other and it's a good environment.

questions.q: Is there anything special that rural students bring to the classroom that helps them academically?
questions.a: I think they definitely—just thinking of all my kids kind of as a whole—they are very independent outside of school. So many of them live out on the back roads, and when you grow up that way, you have a lot to do outside of school. A lot of them, even as eighth and ninth graders, have little jobs in addition to sports, and it's all kind of seasonally related to whatever's going on in the area. In the wintertime if it's hunting season, they all work at some sort of deer processing place, whether it's Wilson's or someone who does it outside of their home, or a lot of them will work at Swain Ski Resort or just local places. Even those who don't are helping out their families on the farm more—they just have a lot of responsibility outside of school. Compared to just my own experience at J-T, kids now who are going into tenth grade—they're just like these adults, basically. They manage so much with their schedules, with their academics, their sports, their jobs, their friends—skills that I didn't develop until I was in college. That makes it sometimes more challenging, because for the kids who maybe don't love algebra or would rather be outside doing something else, they're thinking just like I would after work—I've got to go home and get this done and I have this to-do list on the weekends. After school, I've got work and I've got to help my mom with this and my little sister's got this, and school falls lower on their list, and those are the kids that are harder to reel in. But for a lot of kids, it just makes them more self-directed, more responsible in general. There are kids that I don't have to worry about—they're going to bring an assignment home and bring it back because their time management skills are much more developed at a younger age now because they take on so much. All of their outside-of-school responsibilities tend to fall around something rural for sure—long hours, a lot of physically hard work. I think it gives them a good work ethic for sure.

questions.q: Is there anything else you'd like to share?
questions.a: Teaching in general is a challenging job. I meet a lot of people who are headed into that field, and on one hand I love to see that because there is definitely a shortage going on right now. On the other hand, every time I hear it I'm just kind of like, are you sure? I really do think that previously what attracted people to the profession was the idea of a good retirement system, summers off, a lot of breaks during the school year. Some of those benefits have kind of diminished over the years—if you're in tier six, the retirement is not what it was. For a lot of people, you don't really have the summer off. Whether you're developing a new curriculum for next year, you're on a committee that meets during the summer, you teach summer school, or you're sick of education but you still need the money so you do landscaping—so many teachers still work over the summer. People kind of view it as glorified babysitting and don't think that people understand I have a master's degree in order to be here. It is a challenging job, and even people who go from subbing day-to-day to a long-term maternity leave where you might be here for three months don't really understand the concept of planning that far ahead, everything that goes into the day-to-day responsibility of a teacher. I wish more people knew that it's a very challenging job. The days off are not because it's not a valuable profession—it's because you really need it. I always encourage people when I hear they're going into education—you really, really, really have to want to do it. You really have to enjoy being around kids. You really have to want to help them. You see a lot of teachers who don't enjoy it, or who are annoyed by the kids. Of course there are days that are annoying for sure, but if you're someone who by your third year is kind of like, ugh, I hate these kids, I can't wait for the end of the year—that's not going to bring you joy in your life. Related back to the rural piece—I love working here. I've only worked here. I hope I only ever do work here. I love these kids. I love the class sizes. But I think there is a massive shift going on in education towards regional high schools. Guidelines are changing away from Regents exams so that a kid might not have to choose a Regents path to get to graduation—it's called Portrait of a Graduate, so a kid might choose more of a fine arts path over math and science, but somehow we have to make them equally as rigorous. We just can't offer that here. Logistically, there's no way. I'm already over my contractual teaching obligations, so there's no physical way for me to teach more. I don't know how our music department would be able to find something as rigorous as an Algebra II Regents. There's a lot to be worked out, but I think the thinking behind it is that smaller schools, at least at the high school level, will be phased out and will kind of funnel into these big regional schools. I'm very interested to see what the results of that are, because I love the small classes. I think the small class sizes really lead to a higher success rate and I think it feels better for the kids. I think it's what's better for teachers. So I'm definitely anxious about that shift for our state.
